data.title: SEIV: Multi-Factor Active Large-Cap ETF With A 12x P/E Continues To Shine
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value ETF (SEIV), which continues to deliver impressive returns and remains one of the top-ranked large-cap value funds in my fundamentals-driven ETF scoring system. As I discussed in November, SEIV isn't a single-factor fund, and that's evident when evaluating its fundamentals, as it has above-average exposure to the quality, momentum, and sentiment factors. You also won't find many other funds trading at just 12.29x forward earnings, so in short, SEIV has a lot going for it, and that's why I've decided to assign it a "buy" rating today. Please enjoy the read, and as always, I welcome your questions in the comments section afterward.

SEIV Overview

My review in November was education-themed, but the main takeaway from a strategy perspective was that SEIV is not a single-factor fund, which I think is crucial to being able to succeed in multiple market environments. There are plenty of large-cap value funds for you to choose from - 149 in my database, to be exact. However, of the ones with ultra-cheap P/E ratios, you'll normally see significant tradeoffs on other factors. I've listed the ten cheapest in the table below plus some relevant fundamental statistics as of January 24, 2026, and this view quickly highlights how SEIV stands out in a good way.

Most ETFs fail the growth test, as they currently own stocks that, in aggregate, have negative earnings growth over the last three years. For the ones that do a little better on growth, you'll notice that their margins are on the low side, they lack exposure to the momentum factor, or their expense ratios are far too high. In contrast, SEIV strikes a nice balance on all measures. Its 0.15% expense ratio is the second-lowest in this group, its 3.86% 3Y E​PS CAGR demonstrates some earnings durability, its 19.53% E​BIT margins are above average, and its 27.42% one-year total return is the best in this group. In fact, SEIV finished 2025 as the second-best-performing large-cap value ETF behind the iShares MSCI USA Value Factor ETF (VLUE), and its three-year total return through December 2025 ranks #4/95.

The SEIV website and my educational article contain additional information about its strategy, but the most important takeaway for readers is to treat it as a multi-factor fund. Again, I think this approach is smart and, in a certain way, humble. If I knew which factor the market was going to favor each year, I could easily identify the right ETFs to buy. However, that's not realistic, so the next best thing is to merely increase your chances of success in any environment by ensuring no factor is left behind.

SEIV Performance

Before we move into the evidence of SEIV's multi-factor approach in action, I want to quickly touch on its performance, as that could also help identify potential strengths and weaknesses in its strategy. History is limited, since SEIV only launched on May 18, 2022, but results from June 2022 to December 2025 were excellent. The performance statistics table below also includes other top performers over this period, including the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), the Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (PVAL), and the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV). As a benchmark, I'll also profile the SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV).

SEIV's 15.98% annualized return wasn't the best, but just as important were its inferior risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) and "worst 12 months" figures compared to CGDV and PVAL. In fairness, drawdowns were limited during this 3.5-year period, so this might not be the best way to assess downside risk. However, I think it's something we need to watch for, and I'll offer some insights shortly based on its fundamentals and composition.

I also want to point out that SEIV's relatively poor downside risk-adjusted returns were limited to 2022. As summarized below, the fund declined by 11.5% and 10.0% in June and September 2022, finishing up that partial year with an 8.53% total loss. Since then, returns have been steady, with its 27.42% total return in 2025 doubling up on SPYV.

SEIV Analysis

SEIV Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

We can partially attribute SEIV's success to date to its tech sector exposure, currently at 30.51%. This is 9.93% more than the group average, and SEIV also overweights Communication Services by 6.86%. Top holdings in this sector include Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Verizon Communications (VZ), AT&T (T), and Fox Corp. (FOXA).

In contrast, SEIV underweights Energy and Industrials by 4.85% and 5.83%, respectively. Underweighting energy is a great example of the discipline a multi-factor approach forces. To illustrate, the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) currently trades with an 18.33x forward P/E, the third-cheapest S&P 500 Index sector ETF. However, its margin and capital efficiency ratios are low, and its 3Y E​PS C​AGR is -15.90%, reflecting how difficult it is to forecast earnings in commodity-driven sectors. Limiting exposure to this sector aligns with my long-term outlook that favors stability, and I also think it aligns well with the conservative nature of many value investors.

SEIV Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for SEIV's top 20 holdings, which comprised 45.79% of the fund as of January 24, 2026. Please have a scan of these and the summary statistics at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. SEIV's three-year portfolio beta of 1.03 is somewhat high for a large-cap value fund. While beta only measures market risk, this figure, coupled with the higher standard deviation figures highlighted earlier, suggests SEIV probably isn't the best fund for investors focused primarily on risk management. SEIV's 2.00% Index yield, which I estimate will net investors 1.85% after factoring in the fund's 0.15% expense ratio, also isn't very high, but I think it's solid for a multi-factor fund.

2. SEIV's forward and trailing P/E ratios are 12.29x and 15.62x, respectively, which are substantially lower than the four other ETFs listed. It's interesting that four funds can produce similar results using different strategies, and that opens up the door to owning several of these ETFs to effectively differentiate by manager. SEIV's overlap with CGDV, PVAL, and FDVV ranges between 12% and 19%, and the added diversification from this approach should reduce some of the risk identified in point #1.

3. SEIV has two advantages right now. The first is excellent momentum factor exposure, as its constituents' share prices are up 23.44% over the last six months compared to only 5-11% for the other four ETFs. This is further supported by its components' aggregate 11.38% earnings surprise, which I use as a key input in sentiment analysis. The second is a huge differential between its components' three-year E​PS C​AGR and what analysts currently estimate for E​PS growth over the next twelve months. I call this "investing in turnaround stocks," and it's a common characteristic in many funds I follow. As shown, PVAL is executing this strategy nicely right now (0.83% vs. 9.42%), as is VLUE, the top fund in the category last year.

While I can't say for certain that this strategy is intentional, it is a unique way to promote high-momentum stocks. My only caution is that low-single-digit or negative historical E​PS growth could signal an earnings durability problem where P/E ratios could collapse in an earnings shock. However, SEIV's 3.86% rate isn't too bad - it ranks #95/149 large-cap value ETFs I track. I expect it could still be problematic if market conditions worsen, but I'd also expect its active managers to rotate into more stable stocks. Unfortunately, I don't know if that will happen, given the fund's short history, but in that environment, funds like the WisdomTree U.S. Dividend Growth ETF (DGRW) are probably better bets.

4. Value stocks simply are lower-quality than growth stocks at the moment, and that's easily backed up by fundamental statistics like E​BIT margins, net margins, and return on equity. However, when isolating the analysis only to large-cap value ETFs, SEIV looks slightly better than average. Its E​BIT and net margins of 19.53% and 15.01% are low, but its capital efficiency ratios (R​OE and R​OTC) are still competitive, especially for the value (P/E) offered.

Portfolio X-Ray

In my second point, I suggested owning multiple ETFs as a way to diversify by manager. I encourage you to experiment with your actual holdings using my Portfolio X-Ray Tool, but hypothetically, if you were to own SEIV, CGDV, PVAL, FDVV, and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in equal weight, you could mitigate several of the issues I identified today. Consider these results:

The net result (bottom row) highlights how:

  • Portfolio beta remains unchanged at 1.03.
  • E​BIT margins and return on total capital improve.
  • Historical and estimated earnings growth rates increase.
  • The historical and estimated earnings growth differential declines.

You'll have to decide if the increase in forward P/E (17.85x vs. 12.29x) is worth it, but in my view, it is. We've already established that these ETFs can deliver similarly high returns without ultra-low valuations, but the key is to recognize in advance that eventually, one of these approaches will fall out of favor. Paying more via a higher P/E to improve your portfolio's fundamentals acts as an additional layer of insurance beyond what SEIV already provides with its multi-factor approach, and this hypothetical portfolio's net 17.85x forward P/E is still very much in value territory, given how the average for the large-cap value category is only a point lower at 16.87x.

Investment Recommendation

This article highlighted SEIV's multi-factor approach to stock selection, which I believe is responsible for its success since its launch in May 2022. My factor analysis revealed strengths in momentum, sentiment, and especially value, given its 12.29x forward P/E ratio. SEIV's growth and quality metrics were also adequate, and based on my knowledge of other ETFs, I'm confident that a hidden advantage is how much higher its components' estimated E​PS growth rates are compared to its historical E​PS growth rates. SEIV's holdings don't necessarily have the stability and consistency compared to those held by ETFs like DGRW, but this strategy works well in risk-on environments, and I expect continued success in 2026 if earnings surprises stay above average.

I don't recommend owning SEIV as your sole large-cap value ETF. The fund is just too new, and it hasn't been tested in a market correction. However, that shouldn't prevent you from considering it as part of a broader portfolio of ETFs. This article highlighted three others to complement SEIV and SPY, a core fund I believe most investors should own. By holding these funds in equal weight, investors would still end up with a value-oriented portfolio with the same 1.03 five-year beta and improved fundamentals. Specifically, it reduces the risk of an earnings shock, in which markets won't emphasize earnings growth history rather than potentially lofty expectations. It's one example of how you can use fundamental analysis to get the exposure that best fits your needs, but regardless, I find SEIV's cheap P/E and average-or-better exposure to other factors unique, so I'm comfortable assigning it a "buy" rating today. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Microsoft Meltdown Fuels AI Fears
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Almost all the line items in Microsoft Corp.’s (MSFT) fiscal second quarter looked great. But the stock melted down the most since 2020 anyway. Why? It all goes back to worries about AI spending.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows Microsoft stock going back 12 months. You can see that MSFT was already losing momentum, with RSI peaking last summer and the shares making a series of lower highs in Q4 2025. But yesterday’s breakdown on massive volume really stands out!

So, what happened? Let’s start with the positives from Microsoft's report. Profit came in at $38.5 billion, or $5.16 per share. That beat analyst estimates. Revenue came in at $81.3 billion. That also beat estimates. Operating income? Same story.

But here's the thing: Capital expenditures soared 66% year-over-year to $37.5 billion. At the same time, sales growth at its Azure cloud computing division slowed just a bit to 38%.

That dredged up lingering concerns that Big Tech is spending oodles of cash on data centers, Artificial Intelligence model development, and human capital…but that all of the upfront investment might not generate enough profit down the road. While Meta Platforms Inc. (META) also reported results – and did just fine – the net effect was for tech stocks to slide. The State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) is now basically unchanged since October.

Clearly, the AI story isn’t going away. But neither are AI spending worries. Keep a close eye on this leadership group – because if it continues to struggle, the markets probably will, too.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com
